sentences.text: And how many years did you live in Lubien?
Well, I don't remember it exactly.
I left about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was 18 years old.
A very small town, a country town.
My father [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] used to work by the summer orchards, and we picked the fruit and we brought it to town and sold it.
And what was life like for you in Lubien?
My school time was no school.
It's whatever I learned, I learned in private.
Not much, but whatever I know, I know it from there.
The brothers were grown up already.
They were much older than we girls were.
As they-- they learned just in the cheder when they were small children.
We learned-- well, then it was Russia-- and Russian, Polish, Jewish, and you know, from the prayer books.
I went-- I learned in Poland, when I was a child, German, too.
As [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I spoke German, Jewish, and Polish then.
When I was in Italy, I learned Italian, I spoke.
When I came to the States here, I was speaking Hebrew, Jewish, Polish, German, and Italian.
Now I forgot the Hebrew and the Italian language.
Now ?] I still speak Polish and German and Jewish.
You see, when I was a child, Poland-- Russia occupied parts of Poland.
Austria had a part from Poland, and Germany has a part of Poland.
When after the war, World War first ended, the every Pole-- Pole took back the parts that belonged to Poland.
Well, that's what I know as a child.
After the First World War, I was already a grownup girl, because when the war broke out, I was 10 years old.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when it ended, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was maybe 14.
I think it took four years of war.
My brothers were in the army.
My older brother us in the army when the war broke out, and the other brother was in Odessa, and he was taken to the army, too, during the war.
But, me, I was 10 years old.
And sure, the Poles were not [INAUDIBLE] country there.
My brother came-- was went back to Russia, because he was lived in Russia.
I went not far from Lubien in a big city.
Well, I took care of children.
Well, you know, I used to go home because it wasn't far with the train.
It took an hour to go home by train.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That I lived in Wloclawek.
I had to make a living.
When you are a child, you are with your parents.
When you grow up, you cannot be with your parents.
You have to-- And Lubien was a very small town.
Well, a friend of mine moved to Danzig.
Danzig is on the border from Poland and Germany, I think.
And she had ordering to come, as I went.
It was the border between Poland and Germany.
Wloclawek was not far from-- from where I was born.
It was not-- it was between Germany and Poland and Austria.
It's so many years, who can remember?
Especially when I was a child.
From there I didn't go home.
I don't think so-- I was home.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was writing letters, and I got letters, but I don't think so.
And my father died when I was about 18, 19 years old.
And then my mother was with my sister, living.
She got-- I used to send her a little bit money, and my sister.
He did-- I don't remember what he did, her husband.
What happened in '39, the war broke out.
The Second World War broke out.
Yeah, the Nazis were already there anyway.
It was Germany, part-- part Poland.
Most of them were German people, they are living.
Well, before, in beginning when I came, it was OK.
But later it started with the Nazis.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did everything that they wanted to do.
We always were in fear, because you never knew when they would break in and arrest you, take you away to the concentration camps.
But the living was OK, just we lived in fear.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then there was rich Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they ran away from Poland.
To work, to make a living.
There was no other way to make a living.
Very Beautiful near the ocean, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the Baltic, the Baltic Ocean.
It was at the border of Poland.
At least with the train a half an hour, we were in Gdynia, Poland.
You know what I mean, port?
A big port, Gdynia and Danzig.
And so people would come to Danzig from the Baltic?
Well, before the war, it was 1938, or about.
It was-- it got very bad with the Nazis.
And the England-- Jew-- English Jews from England sent papers to lots of people.
You have to know how-- have somebody to give your name, and I had a girlfriend, we lived together in the same house.
That was already from when the Nazis were there.
And she went to England, and she found a family for me, and they sent me papers to come to England.
And I made the visa to, I made the passport to finish everything.
The day I wanted-- I had to go to the train, the war broke out and I couldn't go anymore.
It was very-- we couldn't do nothing.
We just had to depend on faith.
Poland, the German occupied Poland soon after the war started.
I really-- oh, I got this.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You know, it's so many years.
Many very important things I would like to remember myself, but I cannot remember it.
I know there was committees from Jewish-- in the synagogue was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] help for the people, for the Jews.
And every day the people used to go to the synagogue there.
I forgot how they called it.
They tried to find places to immigrate.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I-- really it's out of mind.
I just know we were like in-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] surrounded with water, but we just couldn't help ourselves, couldn't do anything.
Couldn't go out, couldn't go anywhere.
And during the day it wasn't so bad.
But at night, we were afraid to go out.
Even you were afraid to be in the house, because most of the time the Nazis broken in the houses at night and took out the Jews.
And mostly they took out men members.
It was very-- very dangerous for men.
From one house that I know, they took out the son and the father, the oldest son and the father at night.
As, you know, women were not so afraid like men.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Because I was going around with the English visa, and I couldn't leave.
So one day I was walking in the street.
I always had my passport with me, because we were afraid.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- I really don't know what makes me think to go to the Italian embassy, ask for a visa.
I-- I don't remember why I did it.
I just went-- went into the consulate, they opened the door, an old man-- a very good looking, you know, a fine looking man with a goatee-- came out and asked me what I want.
I said-- I showed him the passport, and I said I would like to go to Italy.
Maybe from Italy I may be able to go to England.
He took the passport from me and he went into the other room and came out with a visa for six months.
Well, I went home and then right away I went to the synagogue there, to the-- the organization.
When I told them I had a visa, to Italy, everybody went-- made their way to go to the window.
Everybody was on the-- envied me that I was so lucky.
And I don't know they helped me.
What they-- what was the help, what, I don't remember, but I have to make another-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know I had to go to the city hall, which was Nazis.
Wherever you go, you were afraid.
Everything went good, went straight, because  I left, I think it was the 29th of the December.
And they came to the bahnhof.
Bahnhof is the station, train station.
I went in the-- how do you call it?
I was a lone woman, and I rode with them to the border of Italy.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember how I came in Trieste.
It was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] an organization.
They called it the Palestine Organization.
And all the immigrants-- there were a thousand immigrants from Germany, from Austria, from Poland, from Hungary.
It was-- they all-- every day they came there to the organization, and they helped them.
Until May, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 19-- is it interesting?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a Jewish man from Romania.
He arranged an illegal passage to Palestine.
They helped everybody, whoever came in.
With where to live, and where to eat, everything.
